shortAnswerQuestions.question: How did technology deepen the divide between North and South? Use at least two specific examples from the unit.
rubric: Identifies at least two specific technologies or innovations and explains how each one affected the North or South differently
Shows how technology pushed the two regions in opposite directions, with the North becoming more industrial and the South becoming more dependent on slavery
Makes a clear argument about why this growing divide mattered for the country's future
Writes 4-6 sentences with specific details and key vocabulary from the unit
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: Technology made the North and South grow in completely opposite directions, which deepened the divide between them. In the North, the Industrial Revolution brought factories, and inventions like interchangeable parts made manufacturing faster and cheaper. The Erie Canal connected northern cities and helped goods move quickly, which boosted trade and attracted immigrants looking for factory jobs. In the South, the cotton gin made it much faster to clean cotton, but instead of reducing the need for slavery, it actually increased it because planters wanted to grow more cotton to make more money. The South stayed agrarian and became even more dependent on enslaved labor while the North built cities and industries. These different paths made it harder and harder for the two regions to agree on laws, especially about slavery.

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Can one person be both a hero and a villain? Choose one historical figure from this unit and use specific examples to support your answer.
rubric: Chooses one historical figure from the unit and clearly states whether that person can be seen as both a hero and a villain

shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: Andrew Jackson can definitely be seen as both a hero and a villain depending on your perspective. Many Americans at the time saw him as a hero because he was a war hero from the Battle of New Orleans and he fought for the common man by expanding voting rights to all white men, not just the wealthy. However, Jackson was also responsible for the Indian Removal Act, which forced tens of thousands of Native Americans off their land and led to the Trail of Tears, where thousands of Cherokee people died. He even ignored the Supreme Court ruling in Worcester v. Georgia that said the removal was illegal. This shows that one person can do things that help some people while causing terrible harm to others. Looking at both sides helps us understand that history is complicated and that we should think carefully before calling anyone simply a hero or a villain.

typingPassages.title: The Age of Jackson
typingPassages.source: Adapted from History Alive and American Yawp
typingPassages.passage: Andrew Jackson rose to the presidency as a champion of the common white man. His political movement, known as Jacksonian Democracy, expanded voting rights and brought ordinary citizens into government. Jackson used the spoils system to reward loyal supporters with civil servant positions, replacing experienced officials with political allies. His kitchen cabinet of informal advisors had more influence than his actual cabinet. The Election of 1828 was full of mudslinging, with both sides launching vicious personal attacks. Jackson also waged war on the Bank of the U.S., vetoing its recharter because he believed it served the wealthy elite. When South Carolina threatened to secede over a protective tariff, Jackson pushed back hard. The state embraced nullification, claiming it could ignore federal law. Jackson responded with the Force Bill, authorizing military action to enforce the tariff. Meanwhile, Jackson signed the Indian Removal Act, forcing the Five Civilized Tribes from their homelands. The Supreme Court ruled in Worcester v. Georgia that states had no authority over tribal nations, but Jackson ignored the decision. Sequoyah had created a written alphabet for the Cherokee language, and the Cherokee had built schools and a newspaper. None of that mattered. Thousands of Cherokee were forced to march west on the Trail of Tears, and roughly four thousand died along the way. Jackson expanded democracy for some Americans while denying it to others.

typingPassages.title: Westward Bound
typingPassages.source: Adapted from History Alive and American Yawp
typingPassages.passage: By the 1840s, Americans believed in manifest destiny, the idea that God intended them to spread across the entire continent. This belief drove thousands of families to head west in search of new territory and fresh opportunities. The Oregon Trail stretched over two thousand miles from Missouri to the Pacific Northwest. Pioneer families loaded covered wagons and braved months of dangerous travel. South Pass, a wide gap in the Rocky Mountains, made the crossing possible for wagon trains. The slogan '54-40 or fight!' demanded that America claim the entire Oregon country, but diplomacy with Britain set the boundary at the 49th parallel. President James K. Polk was determined to expand the nation from coast to coast. Meanwhile, trouble was brewing in Texas. Stephen F. Austin had led American settlers into Mexican Texas, where they lived alongside Tejanos, the Spanish-speaking people already living there. Tensions between settlers and the Mexican government exploded into the Texas Revolution. At the Alamo, fewer than two hundred defenders fought Santa Anna's massive army to the death. Sam Houston then led the Texians to a stunning victory at the Battle of San Jacinto, and Texas became an independent republic. For nearly ten years Texas governed itself, but in 1845 the United States voted to annex the young republic. Texas became the 28th state, but Mexico was furious. The stage was set for an even larger conflict over western land.

typingPassages.title: War, Land, and the Slavery Question
typingPassages.source: Adapted from History Alive and American Yawp
typingPassages.passage: After the United States annexed Texas, a border dispute ignited the Mexican-American War. Mexico insisted the Texas boundary ended at the Nueces River, while America claimed the border lay further south at the Rio Grande. President Polk sent troops into the disputed zone, and when Mexican forces fired on them, Congress declared war. American settlers in California seized the moment and declared the short-lived Bear Flag Republic before the U.S. military arrived. The war ended in 1848 with the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. Mexico surrendered a massive stretch of land known as the Mexican Cession, including present-day California, Nevada, and Utah. A few years later, the Gadsden Purchase completed the southern border of the continental United States. But the new territory raised a dangerous question: would these lands become a free state or a slave state? The Wilmot Proviso tried to ban slavery in all territory taken from Mexico, but it failed in the Senate. The Compromise of 1850 admitted California as a free state and included the Fugitive Slave Act, which required Northerners to help capture escaped enslaved people. The law outraged many in the North. Harriet Beecher Stowe published Uncle Tom's Cabin in 1852, showing readers the brutal reality of slavery. The book turned thousands of Northerners against the institution. America had gained an enormous amount of land, but the fight over slavery in the new territories was tearing the nation apart.

typingPassages.title: A Nation Divided by Industry and Reform
typingPassages.source: Adapted from History Alive and American Yawp
typingPassages.passage: By the mid-1800s, the North and South had become two very different Americas. The Industrial Revolution transformed the Northern economy as factories and railroads reshaped cities. Wealthy industrialists built manufacturing empires, and Eli Whitney's idea of interchangeable parts made mass production possible. The Erie Canal connected the Great Lakes to New York City, slashing shipping costs and fueling economic growth. Meanwhile, the South remained deeply agrarian. Large plantations grew cash crops like cotton, tobacco, and rice using the forced labor of enslaved people. Whitney's cotton gin had made cotton hugely profitable, which tragically increased the demand for enslaved labor. The Tredegar Iron Works in Richmond showed that Southern industry was possible, but most wealth stayed tied to agriculture. Push factors like poverty in crowded Eastern cities and pull factors like the promise of cheap land drove families westward, accelerating deforestation as settlers cleared ancient forests for farms and lumber. At the same time, powerful reform movements were rising. At the Seneca Falls Convention of 1848, Elizabeth Cady Stanton presented the Declaration of Sentiments, demanding equality and voting rights for women. Sojourner Truth, a formerly enslaved woman, gave powerful speeches connecting the fight against slavery to the fight for women's rights. These reformers challenged the nation to live up to its ideals. But the growing divide between the industrial North and the plantation South was becoming impossible to ignore.

funFacts.text: The Oregon Trail was about 2,000 miles long, and it took most wagon trains four to six months to travel the entire route.
During the California Gold Rush, a single egg could cost the equivalent of $25 in today's money because food was so scarce near the mining camps.
funFacts.source: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

funFacts.text: Davy Crockett really did use the motto 'Be always sure you are right, then go ahead' in his autobiography, published in 1834.
funFacts.source: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

funFacts.text: The Alamo was originally a Spanish mission called Mission San Antonio de Valero, built in the 1700s for converting Native Americans to Christianity.
Eli Whitney's cotton gin could clean 50 pounds of cotton per day, replacing the work of many laborers. This made cotton hugely profitable and tragically increased the demand for enslaved labor.
funFacts.source: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

funFacts.text: The phrase 'Manifest Destiny' was first coined by newspaper editor John O'Sullivan in 1845 to justify American expansion westward.
The Seneca Falls Convention of 1848 was the first women's rights convention in the United States. About 300 people attended, including 40 men.
Sam Houston served as president of the Republic of Texas, then later as a U.S. senator and governor of Texas after it became a state.
funFacts.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission

funFacts.text: The Cherokee Nation's forced removal, known as the Trail of Tears, resulted in the death of approximately 4,000 Cherokee people during the 800-mile journey.
James K. Polk is one of the few presidents who accomplished all of his major campaign promises. He pledged to serve only one term and kept that promise.
Before the invention of the cotton gin in 1793, separating cotton seeds from cotton fiber by hand took about 10 hours to produce one pound of clean cotton.
funFacts.source: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

funFacts.text: The word 'Texas' comes from the Caddo word 'tejas,' meaning 'friends' or 'allies.'
Frederick Douglass taught himself to read as a child by trading bread to white children in exchange for reading lessons.
The Battle of the Alamo lasted 13 days. The final assault by Mexican forces on March 6, 1836, was over in less than 90 minutes.
During the Gold Rush, San Francisco's population exploded from about 1,000 in 1848 to over 25,000 by 1850.
Sojourner Truth was born into slavery in New York and didn't gain her freedom until 1826. She went on to become one of the most powerful voices for abolition and women's rights.
